_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Charles City County, VA. Record survival for one of Virginia's original shires (1634) — records survive from an unusually early date despite Civil War damage.
display_name: Charles City County, Virginia — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Charles City, VA — Route 5 at the courthouse village

previous_courthouses.location: Various locations along the James River; courts met in taverns and private houses before a permanent courthouse was built
previous_courthouses.fate: Early court locations are uncertain. Records from this era survive at the Library of Virginia.

previous_courthouses.location: Charles City Court House village on Route 5
previous_courthouses.fate: The antebellum courthouse was damaged during the Civil War but not burned. Records were scattered during the Peninsula Campaign and Seven Days Battles (1862). Some were recovered; others were not.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Peninsula Campaign and Seven Days Battles
record_losses.description: Charles City County was in the direct path of McClellan's advance and the subsequent Seven Days Battles (June-July 1862). Harrison's Landing on the James River became a massive Union encampment. The courthouse was not burned, but records were scattered during military operations. Some volumes were removed by Confederate officials and later returned to the Library of Virginia; others were lost.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Charles City County's records survive from an extraordinarily early date — deeds from 1655, wills from 1665 — but Civil War damage means that some records from all periods have gaps. If {ancestor_name}'s record is missing, the war is the most likely cause.
record_losses.source: Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.

deeds.completeness: Good for a Virginia Tidewater county. Deed books survive from 1655 — among the earliest county-level deed records in Virginia.
deeds.gaps: Some gaps in the 1680s and scattered volumes with war damage. Generally continuous from 1655 onward.
deeds.repository: Charles City County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com VA land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Wills survive from 1665. Estate records more fragmentary.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers lost during the Civil War. Bound will books are largely intact.
wills_estates.repository: Charles City County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds and licenses from the mid-18th century. Earlier marriages documented in church records (Westover Parish).
marriages.gaps: No bonds survive before 1762. Church records partially fill the gap.
marriages.repository: Charles City County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Fragmentary in early decades. More continuous after 1700.
court_minutes.gaps: Significant gaps in order books from the Civil War period.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; Charles City County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events in church records and court orders.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

substitute_sources.description: Westover Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) served Charles City County. Parish register fragments and vestry minutes survive, providing baptism, marriage, and burial records that supplement the civil record.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; Virginia Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish records provide family relationship evidence for the colonial period, especially for marriages before 1762 when county marriage bonds do not survive.
substitute_sources.source: Chamberlayne, C. G., ed. The Vestry Book and Register of Bristol Parish, Virginia. Richmond, 1898.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Slave schedules (1850–1860) document the slaveholding plantation families along the James River corridor.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families, especially the large enslaved populations on the James River plantations (Berkeley, Shirley, Westover).
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Charles City County, VA.

substitute_sources.description: The great James River plantations maintained private records — account books, slave lists, correspondence — that supplement the public record. Some collections survive in archives.
substitute_sources.repository: Virginia Historical Society; Library of Virginia; individual plantation archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Plantation records may document enslaved individuals by name where county records do not. They also reveal economic relationships between planters, tenants, and neighboring yeoman farmers.
substitute_sources.source: Various plantation collection finding aids at the Virginia Historical Society.

narrative_hooks: Charles City County's deed books reach back to 1655 — if {ancestor_name} bought or sold land here in the colonial period, the record almost certainly survives, a paper trail older than most American courthouses.

The Civil War scattered some of Charles City County's records but did not destroy the oldest volumes — {ancestor_name}'s deed from {year} likely survived the same war that turned the county's plantation corridor into a battlefield.

If {ancestor_name} was enslaved on one of the great James River plantations — Berkeley, Shirley, Westover — the plantation's private records may contain {pronoun_possessive} name where the county records hold only the slaveholder's.

Westover Parish's register may record {ancestor_name}'s baptism or burial when the county courthouse has no record at all — in colonial Virginia, the church was the government's local arm, and its records sometimes outlasted everything else.
source_references: Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond: The Peninsula Campaign. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Chamberlayne, C. G., ed. The Vestry Book and Register of Bristol Parish, Virginia. Richmond, 1898.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Charles City County, VA.
